turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Of course, it's clear the statute would apply in those cases. But are you suggesting there is no Federal interest in how Federal prisoners are treated when they're housed in State facilities?

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: Not at all, Your Honor. I am saying that there - - I do recognize that there is a Federal interest in how the prisoners are treated. And - and I think that could be vindicated probably in a properly worded statute that is directed to that. But this statute is not directed to that. Additionally, if I may point out, the government's interest is -- is probably satisfied if, for example, it makes sure that the grant that it's giving or - - or the Federal funds that - - that it is awarding are to an organization that, for example, shares it views -- shares its views or its policies.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But our -- our inquiry, really --we think probably that Congress is the one to decide whether the government's interest is satisfied. So I don't think there's any separate inquiry. If -- if the statute covers this, then the government's interests would be satisfied by an application of the statute, I take it.

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: Well, yes, it would be, Justice Rehnquist, Mr. Chief Justice. It would be, but it's our position that the purpose of this statute is a protection of property statute. That's what this statute is directed at. That's the focus of it. That is what the government is -- is trying to take care of.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Then why -- why -- why is this statute -- I gather the government gave 860 -- or $850,000, and about 915,000 a year, to house 100 -- 100 Federal prisoners -- up to 100.

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That's about $9,000 or $10,000 a prisoner.

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: Yes, Justice Breyer.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: To create a certain kind of a room, a cell.

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And now that room or cell was misused. So why doesn't it affect the -- exactly the -- the very, very expenditure that the Federal Government made? We'll give you a thous -- we'll give you this prisoner, $10,000, to create a certain kind of cell that is to be used in a certain kind of way. And now, this -- your client took a bribe to use it in a different way. So why does that not directly affect the Federal expenditure that was at issue?

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: Well, because in -- in -- in the trial court level, the judge faced the issue of whether this conduct in this case, the -- the conjugal visits or the contact visits, were -- were illegal. And the jury was not asked to find whether that was improper conduct in this situation. And what they were asked were whether bribes were paid for contact visits. The -- the purpose of the statute, as we see it, is a protection of property statute. We see it from -- from the internal structure of the statute.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I'm --I'm sorry, I didn't -- I didn't quite grasp the -- the answer to your question. Is there -- is there a doubt, is it disputed that conjugal visits are unauthorized for -- for Federal prisoners housed in this jail?

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: Yes -- yes, ma'am.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So why would that have to be put to the jury? That's a given, right?

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: No -- no, ma'am. The -- the judge, in instructing the jury, said that the question of whether there were conjugal visits or contact visits -- and that was disputed in the evidence - - was not the issue. The question was, was their payment for these visits, whatever their nature may have been.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, why would there have been a need for the briber to bribe if the conjugal visits were lawful?

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: I -- I think it's because it was a policy of the sheriff's department not to allow conjugal visits. And -- and, of course, the sheriff indicated that the visits that occurred were not conjugal -- conjugal visits. Although they were very liberal, as far as the number of contact visits that were allowed, he denied that conjugal visits occurred.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, wouldn't there also be a Federal interest in - - in not wanting its - - its contract jails to be used for bribery, even if in fact the briber gets a very bad deal?

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: Yes, I -- I think there is --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So, if that's the case, then what difference does it make whether, in fact, these visits were, in fact, lawful or not lawful?

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: Well, because this statute is directed to the protection of property. That is the -- the structure of the statute. That is the history of the statute. For example, this -- this statute is not limited to bribery. It is -- it also deals with theft of government property. It deals with embezzlement of government funds. And -- and it's talking about something -- something of value, something monetary, something that can be measured, being taken away as a result - - or being given out - - the government largess being given out as a result of a bribe given to a government official. That didn't occur --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, suppose -- suppose the jail official charged with purchasing the food for the prisoners is taking a -- a bribe and a kickback, and siphons off $10,000 a month. And, as a result, the prisoners get much worse food. series of transactions." That does seem to to call to mind that there have been a deal. Transaction doesn't -- I wouldn't call assigning someone to a cell a transaction

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: I -- I wouldn't either, and even - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: - - or a business.

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: That's right. I -- I don't see it that way, Your Honor. We feel, Justice Scalia, that the statute applies when -- for example, more appropriately, let's someone comes to an official, who has the power to disburse Federal funds or Federal largess, and -- and they pay him something, and then he gives the contract.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: He gives the contract or he gives --

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: A bid.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: --or gives the business --

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: Or the grant.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or the grant or whatever, yeah.

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: Or things of that nature.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Are you saying that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, why isn't the transaction the housing of Federal prisoners under the contract with the government? It's in connection with the agreement to house Federal prisoners. agreed to house the Federal prisoners. This Petitioner worked at the jail. And he agreed to take money to allow contact visits.

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: That's right. But it -- it didn't have anything to do with, for example, whether he should be in the jail or not. That was something that was strictly the marshal's -- the U.S. marshal's decision. He's the one that decides how many people -- Federal prisoners -- are going to be in that jail or whether they're going to be housed in that jail or another jail.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, the reason the government wants a statute like this is to avoid the unseemliness of giving large sums of Federal money for services to the Federal Government that are being carried out in a corrupt fashion by the entity and the employees charged with conducting it.

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: Yes, ma'am. And -- and, Justice O'Connor, I don't think that -- that that's the basis for this particular statute. The basis for this statute is to protect property, to avoid funds leaving the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, but the language of (B) is broader than that. I mean (A) talks about property; (B) doesn't.

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I mean, we -- we look at the language 2 MR. ENRIQUEZ: Yes. And -- and I think we also

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And this is surely a nonbusiness transaction.

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: -- Justice Stevens, yes. But then you would have a situation where, within the bribery context, there's a portion of the bribery statute that says that the bribe can be of any value - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yeah, but the -- but the transaction has to have value of more than 5,000. They said the visit was worth more than 5,000, using market value in the -- in the relevant market area.

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. --

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: -- Justice Stevens --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- Mr. Enriquez, you --

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- you would call allowing somebody a conjugal visit in exchange for a bribe a transaction. But that's not what they said was the transaction here.

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: No. That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What they called a transaction was allowing the conjugal visit. And that does not seem to me a common way in which to use the word "transaction."

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: And I think it --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Doesn't "transaction" usually mean a deal between two people?

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: And --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Transaction?

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: Yes, sir. And -- and the statute seems to be stretching that definition to fit this set of circumstances.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It seems to be stretching it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what does -- what does the legislative history say?

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: The legislative --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I mean, and specifically on the point of

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, the legislative history --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: --as you're reading --as you are reading it.

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It's like a commercial bribery statute. And that's it. When they have a transaction in the government -- they're awarding contracts or whatever it is -- that's what you look to. The opposite in that will immunize --

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- and misuse of government property?

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: Right. creation of Section 666. And Section 201 is referred to Federal money to un -- unauthorized purposes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: When it says it's aimed precisely, that doesn't mean it's only aimed.

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: That -- that apparently is the intent of this statute. I see it as a protection of property statute.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, how about the title? It says theft or bribery concerning programs receiving Federal money. This is a program receiving Federal money --

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: Yes, ma'am.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- and it's bribery. It just fits like a glove, if you read (B).

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: Well, it -- it -- it does, Your Honor, if we ignore the structure, I think, of the statute in its entirety, and we ignore the fact that concerning the bribery, what amount --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You mean if you ignore the literal language in the title, you have an argument, is that it?

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: No -- no. Additionally, within the bribery -- the bribery provisions, itself, it has two elements in there. What is the amount of the bribe? It can be any amount. And, then, what does it have to deal with? It has to deal with a transaction involving $5,000 or more. And -- and that is something that is carried out throughout the terms of the statute, not only in this 2 embezzlement provisions. 15 somebody is accused of a conspiracy to commit robbery. it varies from statute to statute, crime to crime, as to what the specific intent is; and, in some instances, you probably do. I know that the government has cited the Pinkerton case. That has been rejected in the context of -- of RICO. But, regardless, the argument of whether you bring - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Who -- who -- who has rejected it in the context of RICO?

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: It has been rejected by the government's own decisions in, for example, the Neapolitan case, where it held against our position, but they discussed the idea of whether Pinkerton would apply in the RICO conspiracy context, and the court -- the court --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what court was -- was the Neapolitan?

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: It was the 7th Circuit, Your Honor. And -- and, in addition, in that discussion, they pointed out that the Department of Justice manual indicates that Pinkerton should not be used in this context. Additionally, if we get into the -- the -- the question of traditional conspiracy law, which rules do we import from traditional conspiracy law into what is admittedly a new and drastic statute? I think if we look, associated with the enterprise, but, rather, you've got to participate by management or control of the enterprise. And so, in -- in your hypothetical, if they can't get you for RICO substantive, then I think it may be fair that they can't get you for RICO conspiracy. And as a practical matter, in the cases that -- that are charged below, I -- I -- I've never seen a case where they don't charge RICO substantive and RICO conspiracy also, anyway.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You can -- you can --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The reason you have to be a manager is -- is because the statute requires that you participate directly or indirectly in the conduct of such enterprise's affairs.

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: That's correct, Justice Scalia.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And it's the word "conduct" that -- that brings in that requirement, not -- not any -- any -- any implication that you're not liable for the acts of coconspirators.

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. But -- but that, again -- that is true -- but, again, that incorporates some pretty stunning language in the Pinkerton case. The Pinkerton case being, of course, where someone was charged with a conspiracy, and they proved a conspiracy, but because somebody else committed a substantive crime, he was also charged and found guilty of that substantive crime under the partnership of crime theory. And -- and that's been highly criticized and -- and criticized even by the cases that -- that support the government's position.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But the government doesn't need that part of the Pinkerton case here. It seems to me it just needs what -- what you apparently agree with the common law idea. That if you agree that a robbery should be committed, the agreement doesn't have to provide for you personally com -- committing the robbery.

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: As I indicated, Mr. Chief Justice, I -- I think that it depends on a crime-to-crime basis. I don't think that, across the board --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, how about robbery? Take that for a crime.

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: Well, I think it's a specific intent, and you have to agree that -- that a robbery is going to be committed, and you've got to do something in furtherance of it. Drug crimes --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But -- but are - are you -- are you saying that you would have to personally commit the robbery in a situation like that?

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: No. I -- I think agree to commit elements of the robbery, yes. If it please the Court, I would like to reserve my remaining time for rebuttal.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Very well, Mr. Enriquez. Mr. Wolfson, we'll hear from you.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: The first statute before the Court, the bribery offense, which is stated in Section 666, specifies four elements. First, the defendant must be an agent of an entity that receives Federal benefits. Second, the Federal benefits received by that entity must exceed $10,000 in a 1-year period. Third, during that 1-year period, the defendant must accept something of value, intending to be influenced in a transaction of the entity. And, fourth, the transaction must involve something of value.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What was the transaction?

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: The transaction in this case was allowing con -- was allowing conjugal visits. That was the theory on which the case was submitted to the jury. The jury instruction is found on page 127 of the joint appendix. And the judge says, now -- says, now, what are the transactions? The transactions are allowing contact 15 a, you know, a flavor of a deal between two people -- 15 window. SUITE 400 15 MR. WOLFSON: Well, I -- 15 MR. WOLFSON: Well, the case was certainly a State, and not a Federal, prisoner?

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Yes, it -- it would. It -- it's because the -- the -- all of the four elements of the offense are -- are stated in the hypothetical --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And because the -- because the transaction is that there's a previous Federal grant to the prison?

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: No, no. The -- the trans -- the transaction is - - let's suppose there was a State --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What's the -- what's the Federal grant transaction that's involved in - -

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: The Federal grant --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- in Justice Ginsburg's hypothetical?

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: The Federal --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The transaction, you have told me, is the conjugal visit, which is not -- is not related to the Federal grant.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Well, the Federal -- the Federal Government is giving -- on the hyp -- I -- I hope I'm understanding the hypothetical correctly -- the Federal Government is giving the jail money to house a Federal prisoner. Now, it so happens that at - - during that 1-year period, there is a State prisoner in the jail cell next to Beltran -- SUITE 400 criminal statute

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you consider the Justice Department a business, Mr. Wolfson?

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Excuse me?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do you consider the Justice Department a business?

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: I think that the business -- it could be said that the business of the Justice Department is to -- is to bring cases -- the business of --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It could be said analogously, I think - -

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- but I wouldn't -- that wouldn't be the normal use of "business." I don't --

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: It's the -- but it's the business --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- regard Federal agencies as being in business.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: It is -- what it says, it is the business or transaction of -- of an -- of an organization, agency or government. And it doesn't --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I'd like to get just one point clear. Are -- are you saying that, under this statute, business transaction or series of transactions can be, depending on the way the government charges in the indictment, either SUITE 400

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Well, the -- the question -- the question presented is clearly whether there has to be a nexus shown to the treatment of -- well, it says what kinds of cases involving State employees are subject to prosecution under 18 U.S.C. 666. But then it goes on to say, do such cases include cases where no Federal funds are di - - disbursed or impinged.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right. Right.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Now, having said that, I think that the statute -- I mean, the statute clearly covers cases like this one, where there is a strong Federal interest in the integrity of - - there is an - - there is an important Federal interest in cases like this, where the -- the recipient of the Federal funds is housing Federal prisoners, is responsible for their custody and their safekeeping and is obligated to -- to keep them under certain conditions, but also to keep them under certain privileges. And --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, what -- what if -- what if Congress had simply provided for grants to allow the State prisons, without requiring that the house State prisoners. Now, would this statute still apply? With -- with Federal prisoners.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: If Congress -- I mean, if Congress gave the Federal - - gave the States or local governments just money to house State prisoners, yes, I think that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The statute would still apply.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Yes. The statute does not require a nexus to the implementation of the Federal program.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Nor does it require any special Federal interest.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: I think that's correct, on the terms of the statute. I think, though, that -- I mean, Congress could, for example, give a local government a -- an unrestricted block grant, sort of a -- a revenue-sharing grant in aid program. And I think that Congress could -- under the Spending Clause, Congress could have restrictions such as this follow the grants and -- the grants and their funds.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So that for the next 30 years, any bribery -- if it's more than $5,000 involved -- in conjunction with the ordinary, day-to-day running of a prison that's been built with Federal monies, is under this statute - -

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: No, the bribe has to - - the bribe has to take place during the -- the 1-year --a 1-year period surrounding the -- the receipt of Federal benefits. So it's not -- it's not as though once you get Federal -- once you get $10,000 of Federal money, you -- you're on the hook forever. It's -- the -- the statute is --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, subsection (B) says that the statute applies where the organization, government or agency, gets, in any 1-year period --

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- benefits in excess of $10,000 from the Federal Government.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Right. But 1-year period is then defined -- this is on page 3a of our -- the appendix in our brief -- the term, "in any 1-year period" means a continuous period that commences no earlier than 12 months before the commission of the offense and ends no later than 12 months after the commission of the offense. So - - I mean, it has -- the bribe has to be roughly contemporaneous with the -- with the receipt of Federal funds. And the -- so, after the 1-year period is over, if the agency doesn't get or take Federal funds --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That doesn't say that. I don't -- I don't read the 1-year period to impose that requirement. It just describes -- that just defines the period in which the $10,000 or more has to be received. It doesn't say anything about when the crime has to be committed.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Before the commission of the offense.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Oh.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Yeah. I mean, it's -- you see, the statute --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Oh, I see.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: -- is -- is somewhat of a -- of a -- of an acrostic. If you -- if you look at Section (B) -- well, let's -- let me start with Section (A). Whoever -- if the circumstance described in Section (B) exists, then you have to look at (B). And it says, the circumstance referred to in subsection (A) is that the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Beautiful.

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: -- is that the organization received benefits in any 1-year period. And then you go on to (D)(5), and it says what the term "in any 1-year period" means. So, although --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So that is related to the time of the offense?

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: Right. That's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And if I read it correctly, you -- you -- you can take a bribe, and if you're unfortunate enough that the prison you take the bribe from, later, within less than 1 year after you commit the offense, gets Federal money, they got you under the statute; is that right? You -- you are retroactively --

turns.name: PAUL R. Q. WOLFSON
turns.text: I think that is - - I think that is - -

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- sucked into the vortex of -- 2 itself, is directed to the heads of the enterprises, the people that manage or control. Ernst -- Reves v. Ernst & Young indicates that -- that that's the idea. So it -- it just doesn't seem unfair that if you can't get them under RICO substantive, that there's apparently a great discussion in the legislative history that -- that would indicate, well, we -- we can get them under RICO conspiracy. The -- the focus here is, what is the object of the conspiracy? And the object of the conspiracy, in -- in our view, is not simply to join an enterprise that -- that operates in that fashion, but to -- to personally agree to commit two specific acts of racketeering. That's what the whole discussion in the legislative history was about, not limited RICO substantive or RICO conspiracy -- just to when the statute would or would not apply. I would suggest to the Court, concerning both of these statutes, Section 666 and -- and the RICO statute, that there are certain areas of government which historically this Court has indicated to us, under the Constitution, have been deemed within the province, or primarily within the province, of States. For example, education, domestic relations, State officials and criminal law. This case has two of those areas: State 15 Government can do that, ma'am, if -- if it is couched in SUITE 400 SUITE 400 it could be justified only on that basis.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But the Tenth Amendment argument that you're presenting to us now --

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: Yes, ma'am.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: -- did you make that in the lower courts?

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: We didn't specifically come out and say Tenth Amendment, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Enriquez. The case is submitted.

turns.name: FRANCISCO J. ENRIQUEZ
turns.text: Thank you.
